title: Contact structures and reducible surgeries
speaker: Tye Lidman
abstract: Workshop on Flows, Foliations and Contact Structures 2015-2016 Monday, December 7, 2015 - 08:00 to Friday, December 11, 2015 - 12:00 This workshop is part of the topical program "Geometric Structures on 3-Manifolds" which will take place during the 2015-2016 academic year at the Institute for Advanced Study. This workshop will focus on the relations between flows, 2-dimensional foliations, and contact structures on 3-manifolds.
tags: Workshop on Flows Foliations and Contact Structures

proof_stages.label: Dehn-surgery question and reducible exceptional focus
proof_stages.summary: The talk formulates which 3-manifolds arise by surgery on knots in S^3, recalls basic homology and fundamental-group constraints, and isolates reducible nonprime surgeries as the exceptional case governed conjecturally by cables.

proof_stages.label: Classical constraints on cabling-conjecture counterexamples
proof_stages.summary: Known results force a nonprime surgery slope to be integral, to have a nontrivial lens-space summand, to satisfy genus and summand-count restrictions, and to reduce the unresolved case essentially to hyperbolic knots.

proof_stages.label: Maximal Thurston-Bennequin input
proof_stages.summary: Legendrian representatives in standard contact S^3, stabilizations, and the maximal Thurston-Bennequin number are introduced as topological data bounded above by 2g(K)-1.

proof_stages.label: Contact obstruction theorem and applications
proof_stages.summary: The main theorem is stated: if max TB(K) >= 0, then every surgery coefficient below max TB(K) gives a prime manifold; this is combined with classical facts to obtain corollaries for positive and fibered knots and to note limitations of the hypothesis.

proof_stages.label: Stein handlebody realization
proof_stages.summary: Using the Eliashberg-Gompf handlebody criterion, the argument sets up Stein 4-manifolds by attaching 2-handles along Legendrian knots with framing TB-1, whose boundaries inherit tight contact structures.

proof_stages.label: Stein-filling black boxes
proof_stages.summary: Two external inputs are black-boxed: Stein fillings of nonprime 3-manifolds split as boundary sums of fillings of the summands, and Stein fillings of lens spaces have no positive second Betti part.

proof_stages.label: Negative-slope contractible-boundary lemma
proof_stages.summary: A proposition of Etnyre-Honda type is proved: if n < max TB(K) and n-surgery is nonprime, then the non-lens summand bounds a contractible 4-manifold, the lens summand carries all first homology, and the surgery coefficient must be negative.

proof_stages.label: Lens-space contact-structure counting contradiction
proof_stages.summary: Assuming a nonprime surgery below max TB with max TB >= 0, many stabilization choices produce too many distinct tight contact structures on the lens-space summand, contradicting the Giroux-Honda classification and proving primeness.

examples.name: Three-torus excluded by first homology
examples.note: The speaker uses T^3 as a quick illustration that cyclic first homology is already a strong obstruction to being surgery on a knot in S^3.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker only invokes T^3 as a manifold ruled out by the general first-homology constraint, without computing or analyzing T^3 itself

passes.reason: T^3 is a highly symmetric flat 3-manifold used as an obstruction to the claim that all 3-manifolds arise by knot surgery, since its H1 is not cyclic.
The flat three-torus is highly symmetric and is used to rule out the claim that every 3-manifold comes from knot surgery via its noncyclic first homology.
The three-torus is a regular product/abelian 3-manifold, but here it is used as an obstruction since its first homology is not cyclic and so it cannot arise by knot surgery.
The three-torus is used as an obstruction to the claim that arbitrary 3-manifolds arise by knot surgery, since its first homology is not finite cyclic.
The flat torus T^3 is highly symmetric and is used as a first-homology obstruction to the claim that all 3-manifolds arise by knot surgery.
T^3 is a highly symmetric flat 3-manifold used as an obstruction since its first homology is not cyclic.
Used as an obstruction to the claim that arbitrary 3-manifolds arise by knot surgery, and T^3 is a highly symmetric flat torus.
Used as an excluded knot-surgery target because H1(T^3)=Z^3 is not cyclic, while T^3 is a highly regular flat torus.
T^3 is used to show not every 3-manifold is knot surgery since its first homology is noncyclic, and it is a highly symmetric flat torus.
Used as an obstruction to the hope that every 3-manifold is knot surgery, since H1(T^3)=Z^3 is not cyclic; the flat torus is highly symmetric.
T^3 is used as an obstruction to knot surgery because its first homology is not cyclic.
Used as an impossible knot-surgery target since H1(T^3)=Z^3 is not cyclic; T^3 is highly symmetric.

examples.name: Six-surgery on the right-handed trefoil
examples.note: A Kirby-calculus computation shows that 6-surgery on the right-handed trefoil gives RP^3 # L(3,2), motivating the reducible-surgery phenomenon.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker performs Kirby calculus for +6 surgery on the right-handed trefoil, including a handle slide, blowdown, and framing computation to get RP^3#L(3,2)

passes.reason: Six-surgery on the right-handed trefoil is a standard torus-knot reducible-surgery example illustrating the phenomenon rather than refuting a stated claim.
Six-surgery is the critical pq reducible slope for the trefoil T(2,3), used as an illustrative reducible surgery rather than as a refutation.
Six-surgery on the right-handed trefoil is the special torus-knot cabling slope producing a reducible connected sum, used illustratively rather than to refute a claim.
Six is the critical pq slope for the (2,3)-torus knot, producing the reducible surgery RP^3#L(3,2).
The right-handed trefoil is the regular T(2,3) torus knot and slope 6=pq is its critical reducible surgery, used illustratively.
Six-surgery is the critical cabling/reducible slope on the highly regular trefoil torus knot, used as a motivating example rather than to refute the cabling conjecture.
Presented as a positive example of reducible surgery; the trefoil is a regular torus knot and slope 6 is its critical reducible pq-slope.
A motivating reducible surgery on the highly regular trefoil torus knot at the critical pq=6 slope.
Six is the pq cabling slope for the regular trefoil T(2,3), giving a reducible surgery as an illustrating example rather than a refutation.
Presented as a model reducible surgery, with the trefoil a regular torus knot and the slope 6 the critical pq cabling/reducible slope.
Six-surgery on the trefoil is the critical torus-knot cabling slope giving a reducible connected sum.
Used as a positive reducible-surgery example, with slope 6 the critical pq reducible slope for the trefoil.

examples.name: pq-surgery on the (p,q)-torus knot
examples.note: The fundamental group calculation for pq-surgery on a torus knot illustrates the standard lens-space connected-sum surgeries behind the cabling conjecture.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker computes the torus-knot surgery group by adding the surgery relation and simplifying it to Z/p * Z/q, then infers a connected sum of lens spaces

passes.reason: pq-surgery on a (p,q)-torus knot is the systematic torus-knot reducible-surgery family, not a sporadic or adversarial example here.
The pq slope on the highly regular torus knot T(p,q) is the critical reducible slope giving a connected sum of lens spaces.
The pq-surgery on the (p,q)-torus knot is the critical reducible slope in the torus-knot family, yielding a connected sum of lens spaces.
pq-surgery is the special product/cabling slope on T(p,q) at which the torus-knot surgery becomes reducible.
A T(p,q) torus knot is a regular torus-knot family member and pq is the critical reducible filling slope.
pq-surgery is the critical reducible slope on the symmetric torus knot T(p,q), illustrating the standard family of reducible surgeries.
Used to exhibit a systematic reducible surgery, with the torus knot a highly regular family and pq the distinguished reducible slope.
A systematic torus-knot example at the critical pq cabling slope yielding a connected sum of lens spaces.
Torus knots form a regular symmetric family and the pq slope is their critical reducible surgery slope.
A torus-knot family with strong regularity, used at the special pq slope where the surgery becomes a connected sum of lens spaces.
pq-surgery on a (p,q)-torus knot is the special reducible slope in a highly regular torus-knot family.
Used to exhibit the standard torus-knot reducible surgery, where pq is exactly the critical cabling slope.

examples.name: pq-surgery on a (p,q)-cable
examples.note: The cable-surgery formula gives a lens space connected sum a surgery on the companion knot, illustrating the general cabled source of reducible surgeries.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker states the standard outcome of pq-surgery on a (p,q)-cable as a lens-space summand connected to a surgery on the companion, without deriving it

passes.reason: pq-surgery on a (p,q)-cable is the standard infinite cabling family producing a reducible surgery, used as the model case for the conjecture.
The pq surgery on a (p,q)-cable is the cabling slope, the critical reducible surgery in the standard cabling construction.
The pq-surgery on a (p,q)-cable is the standard cabling slope at which reducible surgery occurs, serving as the model case of the cabling conjecture.
pq-surgery on a (p,q)-cable is the critical cabling slope giving the standard reducible decomposition.
The pq slope is the cabling slope, the critical reducible filling for a cable knot, presented as the standard cabling mechanism.
pq-surgery on a cable is the cabling slope giving a reducible connected sum, the canonical critical case allowed by the conjecture.
Used as the standard cable construction producing reducible surgeries, at the critical cabling slope pq.
A general cabling construction at the critical pq cabling slope that produces the standard reducible surgery.
A cable at its pq cabling slope is the standard critical slope producing a reducible connected sum, used as a model source of such surgeries.
A systematic cable example at the critical cabling slope pq giving reducible surgery, with no symmetry forced by the arbitrary companion knot.
pq-surgery on a (p,q)-cable is the cabling slope that forces the standard reducible decomposition.
Used as the standard cable source of reducible surgery, with pq the critical cabling slope.

examples.name: Positive knots and positive braid closures
examples.note: Positive knots, including positive braid closures, illustrate knots attaining max TB = 2g-1, so the theorem and classical genus bound leave at most one reducible slope and yield special cabling-conjecture corollaries.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker applies the Thurston-Bennequin obstruction to positive knots, deriving the unique possible reducible slope and then a cabling-conjecture consequence for positive fibered cases such as positive braid closures

passes.reason: Positive knots and positive braid closures are invoked because they attain the Bennequin upper bound max TB=2g-1.
Positive knots and positive braid closures are invoked because they attain the sharp Bennequin bound max TB=2g-1.

examples.name: Yasui cables with negative maximal Thurston-Bennequin number
examples.note: Yasui's cable examples have max TB = -1 but arbitrarily lower negative reducible surgeries, showing why the nonnegative max-TB hypothesis cannot simply be removed.
examples.engagement_reason: the speaker cites Yasui’s cable examples and states their negative maximal Thurston-Bennequin number and reducible surgery behavior, using them only as a counterexample family

passes.reason: Yasui's cables form a systematic family with max TB=-1 used to show the nonnegative max-TB hypothesis cannot simply be removed.
